name: London: Royal Westminster & The Heart of Power

stops.name: Buckingham Palace
stops.shortDescription: The official London residence of the UK's sovereigns since 1837. It features 775 rooms and is the center of national celebrations and state occasions.

stops.name: St James's Palace
stops.shortDescription: Built by Henry VIII largely between 1531 and 1536, it remains the most senior royal palace in the UK. The accession of a new monarch is still proclaimed from its balcony.

stops.name: Westminster Abbey
stops.shortDescription: Since 1066, this World Heritage Site has been the location of the coronations of 40 English and British monarchs. It is also the final resting place of thousands of notable figures.

stops.name: Houses of Parliament
stops.shortDescription: Also known as the Palace of Westminster, this is the meeting place for both the House of Commons and the House of Lords. The current stunning Gothic Revival structure was built after a disastrous fire in 1834.

stops.name: Big Ben
stops.shortDescription: Big Ben is actually the nickname for the Great Bell of the striking clock, weighing nearly 14 tons. The iconic clock tower itself was officially renamed Elizabeth Tower in 2012 to mark the Queen's Diamond Jubilee.

stops.name: 10 Downing Street
stops.shortDescription: The official residence and executive office of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom. It has served this vital political purpose since 1735 under Sir Robert Walpole.

stops.name: Trafalgar Square
stops.shortDescription: A public square established in the early 19th century that commemorates the Battle of Trafalgar, a British naval victory in 1805. At its center stands Nelson's Column, guarded by four massive bronze lions.

stops.name: The National Gallery
stops.shortDescription: Located right on Trafalgar Square, this prestigious art museum houses a collection of over 2,300 paintings dating from the mid-13th century to 1900.

stops.name: Leicester Square
stops.shortDescription: The pedestrianized heart of London's entertainment district, surrounded by major cinemas hosting global film premieres and bustling with street performers.

stops.name: Chinatown
stops.shortDescription: Marked by its iconic red lanterns and magnificent gates, London's Chinatown is packed with authentic Asian restaurants, bakeries, and vibrant supermarkets.

stops.name: Piccadilly Circus
stops.shortDescription: London's answer to Times Square, famous for its massive illuminated advertising screens and the Shaftesbury Memorial Fountain featuring the statue of Anteros.

stops.name: Covent Garden
stops.shortDescription: A popular shopping and tourist site centered around the elegant Apple Market building. It's world-renowned for its high-end boutiques and licensed street entertainers.

stops.name: Royal Opera House
stops.shortDescription: One of the world's leading opera houses, located just off Covent Garden. The stunning building is home to the Royal Opera, the Royal Ballet, and the Orchestra of the Royal Opera House.

stops.name: Somerset House
stops.shortDescription: A spectacular neoclassical complex on the south side of the Strand overlooking the Thames. It hosts a famous ice rink in winter and beautiful fountains in summer.
